original_reasoning: Akhna - time extended long over him vs 'I have yawned/gaped' - both suggest spreading/opening wide

Arabic جدي=kid, young goat (also constellation Capricorn); جدية=blood-trace, saddle-cloth. Greek aigida=aegis (acc. of aigis), the goatskin shield of Zeus/Athena, derived from aig-/aix=goat. Direct semantic match: both root meanings center on goat/young goat. The Arabic gloss focuses on the 'blood trace' sense which is the secondary meaning, but the core جدي=goat-kid clearly matches Greek aig-=goat. Strong root match through the goat sense.
Arabic gharf = scoop/draw (water), gharif = dense trees; Greek graphos = writer/scratcher. Both from scratching/drawing action — plausible deep root resonance.
Arabic خروج is going out or bringing forth, while Greek is an engraved mark, something cut out or produced on a surface; the action-result link is plausible.

الآبش=الذي يزيّن فناء الرجل وباب داره (one who adorns the courtyard and door of a man's house). βαλβίς=the rope/barrier marking start/finish of a racecourse (also: threshold, starting point). Both involve a marked boundary/entrance point. The doorway/threshold concept connects them.
Variant spelling of Baghdad; shares Iranian baga-data base with Bagadates.
Arabic poison's sharp edge can plausibly align with cutting or engraving through the notion of sharp incision.
Arabic fard = 'single, solitary, doe that left the herd'; Greek = 'gone, fled, departed'. Plausible conceptual echo via separation/solitude.
